title: [CI:DOCS] Add GPG pubkeys used for signing to the Podman repo
body:
These are the public keys used by the members of the Podman team approved to make releases.

A few folks are notably missing, because their keys have expired. They will be added once the situation has resolved itself.

comments.body:
[APPROVALNOTIFIER] This PR is **APPROVED** This pull-request has been approved by: *mheon* The full list of commands accepted by this bot can be found [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands?repo=containers%2Fpodman). The pull request process is described [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process)
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - ~~[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/OWNERS)~~ [mheon] Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment
Thanks! Would it make sense to sign this commit with your key as a form of trust?

We also may want to add some security around the directory - possibly add a label/comment on any commit modifying the keys?

@edsantiago @cevich

> We also may want to add some security around the directory - possibly add a label/comment on any commit modifying the keys?

All changes there should only be made with a valid key from this directory to actually built a chain of trust. And I guess that should be validated in CI to make sure we do not miss it on reviews.
As an alternative, we could create a separate repo under the containers/ org so we can have higher scrutiny of individual changes.
On further thought, separate repo is a better idea, I'll talk with @baude to make it happen

My key was set to never expire, but maybe something somewhere is flagging keys older than 10 years as expired?? Anyway, I resent my key to the ubuntu keyserver (still with `expires: never`). If you still notice trouble with my key, I can set an expiration date and resend.
@lsm5 Looks like it's just a subkey that expired. The main key is still valid.

> @lsm5 Looks like it's just a subkey that expired. The main key is still valid.

hmm, I only have 1 subkey on that key and IIRC that was set to never expire, don't know if I can reproduce that now though. Anyway, no biggie if main key works. Gotta love GPG.

> On further thought, separate repo is a better idea, I'll talk with @baude to make it happen

***And make it a private repo. please :smile_cat:***

For automation, Cirrus-CI is an option assuming management can type in their credit-card number for this.  Benefit there is, we've already got a huge library of VM images, scripts, and expertise/experience using them.

I believe Github Actions works on private repos in a limited fashion, but maybe good enough? 
 However, my experience there is is: The more and more complex/fancy you go, the more "WTF?!?!?!" fundamental design-problems you run into.  But for small/simple things it's perfectly fine.
Clarification: My comment applies to hosting/using the ***private*** keys as an automation-secret.
@cevich where are you suggesting storing the private key?  My knees are trembling a bit thinking about them being out on GitHub at all, private or not.....
Yes, well, it's my bad, likely out of scope for this.  To fast to click, not fast enough to read the subject :laughing:
